title: 6 Dishonoured AFT Transactions and Recourse Period for Business PADs
body_text:
6	Dishonoured AFT Transactions and Recourse Period for Business PADs
6.1	Overview of Dishonoured AFT Transactions and Recourse Period of Business PADs
This Chapter provides the procedures for returning dishonoured AFT Transactions and outlines when a Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution, Form 1640, is used to advise the originating financial institution of a returned AFT Transaction. Although a Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution, Form 1640, isnât required, it is recommended that financial institutions send some form of Written Notice as a courtesy. This Chapter also provides the procedures for advising members of the recourse period for Business PADs.
Related Information
Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution,
Form 1640
6.2	Returning Dishonoured AFT Transactions
A dishonoured (unpostable) personal, business, funds transfer or Cash Management PAD, or Direct Deposit where applicable, can be returned through the Clearing for reasons such as, but not limited to, NSF, stop payment, account closed, etc. The transaction is returned to the financial institution that originally entered the transaction into the Clearing. It must be returned no later than the Business Day following receipt by the first organizational unit of the financial institution that is able to make a decision to dishonour the item.
Return original Credit/Debit Transactions, C/D and E/F records, electronically via ORS or the Batch Return Service. See the Online Return System (ORS) Part and/or Chapter 8, Returning AFT Transactions via the Batch Return Service â BC Only.
Debits that are returned with the codes 901 â NSF (Not Sufficient Funds) or 908 â Funds Not Cleared are returned automatically on a one-time basis for AFT Originators that have the AFT Re-presentment service. See the Introduction to AFT Part for more information.
6.3	Notice of Dishonour
The Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution, Form 1640, is used to advise the originating financial institution of a returned AFT Transaction. The Canadian Payments Association (CPA) doesnât mandate this process, but financial institutions can provide Written Notice of some returned transactions as a courtesy. The transaction itself must still be returned through the Clearing, following regular procedures.
To provide notice of dishonour, complete a Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution, Form 1640. Completion of the form is self-explanatory. See Figure 1, Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution, Form 1640.
Note:	Mail a copy of the form to the originating financial institution on the same day that the item is returned through the Clearing. Retain a copy for the financial institutionâs records.
Figure 1: Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution, Form 1640
Related Information
Notice of Dishonour â Negotiating Financial Institution,
Form 1640
6.4	Advising Members of Business PADs of the Recourse Period
In certain situations, the recourse period for Business PADs is less than that of other PAD types. Financial institutions can notify members of this type of incoming AFT Transaction. Financial institutions can establish a policy of notifying members only when a Business PAD exceeds a threshold dollar value.
See the Business Pre-authorized Debits â AFT (PADB/UADB) report to obtain a list of incoming Business PADs. Contact the member as per the financial institutionâs internal policy.
The financial institution can still be liable to members for a longer time period if the account agreement provides the member with 30 days to dispute items recorded on the account statement.
Related Information
Section 1.3
, Recourse Deadlines
END
